maxContractTerm.value: 5 years (including any renewal periods), unless the procurement official determines in writing — with the determination included in the procurement file — that a longer period is necessary to obtain the procurement item, is customary for industry standards, or is in the best interest of the procurement unit. By its own terms, the cap does not apply to contracts for the design or construction of a facility, a road, a public transit project, or for the financing of equipment (none of which is a private school-bus services contract).
maxContractTerm.citation: Utah Code Ann. § 63G-6a-1204(7)–(8) (Multiyear contracts); see §§ 63G-6a-103(30) ("educational procurement unit" includes a school district), 63G-6a-103(50) ("multiyear contract"), 63G-6a-103(59) ("procurement unit" includes an educational procurement unit) — official text at https://le.utah.gov/xcode/Title63G/Chapter6A/63G-6a-S1204.html and https://le.utah.gov/xcode/Title63G/Chapter6A/63G-6a-S103.html

rebidRequired.label: Required
rebidRequired.rule: A procurement unit may not continue or renew a multiyear contract after the end of the multiyear contract term or the renewal periods described in the contract unless it engages in a new standard procurement process or complies with an exception described in Utah Procurement Code Chapter 6a (e.g., sole source under § 63G-6a-802; emergency procurement under Part 8; small purchase under § 63G-6a-506; cooperative procurement under § 63G-6a-2105; approved vendor list under § 63G-6a-507). The Utah Procurement Code applies to school districts as "educational procurement units." Within the five-year envelope the contract may include statutorily compliant renewal periods (no new procurement needed) per the multiyear-contract definition in § 63G-6a-103(50).
rebidRequired.citation: Utah Code Ann. § 63G-6a-1204(6); see §§ 63G-6a-103(30), (59), 63G-6a-802 (exceptions to standard procurement), 63G-6a-802.7 (limited extension without new procurement) — https://le.utah.gov/xcode/Title63G/Chapter6A/63G-6a-S1204.html

renewalMechanics.value: Within the 5-year multiyear-contract envelope, a school district may include renewal periods that do not require a new competitive procurement; a "multiyear contract" is statutorily defined to include a contract that permits renewal without competition beyond the first year (§ 63G-6a-103(50)). Three statutory mechanics apply: (1) Annual-appropriation contingency — notwithstanding any IFB, RFP, or contract provision to the contrary, a multiyear contract may not continue or be renewed for any year after the first year if adequate funds are not appropriated or otherwise available (§ 63G-6a-1204(3)); federally-funded and partially federally-funded contracts have parallel conditional-renewal rules (§ 63G-6a-1204(4)–(5)). (2) Five-year ceiling on the contract "including any renewal periods," subject to the written-determination exception in § 63G-6a-1204(7). (3) Outside the contract's own renewal terms, the procurement official may extend an existing contract without engaging in a standard procurement process only under the narrow circumstances of § 63G-6a-802.7 (e.g., to avoid lapse in a critical government service or mitigate harm to public health/safety/welfare/property; for the period of a protest, appeal, or court action; generally ≤ 120 days, with longer extensions allowed only after consulting the attorney general or unit's attorney and making a written determination). No statutory CPI / fuel-index / automatic-evergreen-rollover mechanism is prescribed for student-transportation contracts; price-adjustment terms are permissible by the contract-type definition in § 63G-6a-103(35) ("fixed price contract with price adjustment") but the duration limits above still control.
renewalMechanics.citation: Utah Code Ann. § 63G-6a-103(50) ("multiyear contract"); § 63G-6a-103(35) (price-adjustment fixed-price contract); § 63G-6a-1204(3)–(7); § 63G-6a-802.7 (extension without standard procurement) — https://le.utah.gov/xcode/Title63G/Chapter6A/63G-6a-S1204.html and https://le.utah.gov/xcode/Title63G/Chapter6A/63G-6a-S802.7.html

finalFields.statutoryCitation: Primary: Utah Code Ann. tit. 63G, ch. 6a (Utah Procurement Code), particularly § 63G-6a-1204 (Multiyear contracts) (https://le.utah.gov/xcode/Title63G/Chapter6A/63G-6a-S1204.html); § 63G-6a-103(30) ("educational procurement unit" includes a school district, a public school (including a local school board or a charter school), the Utah Schools for the Deaf and the Blind, the Utah Education and Telehealth Network, certain institutions of higher education, and the State Board of Education); and § 63G-6a-103(59) ("procurement unit" includes an educational procurement unit) (https://le.utah.gov/xcode/Title63G/Chapter6A/63G-6a-S103.html). Supporting: § 63G-6a-103(50) ("multiyear contract"); § 63G-6a-802 (exceptions to standard procurement, including sole source and emergency); § 63G-6a-802.7 (extension of a contract without a standard procurement process); § 63G-6a-2105 (cooperative procurement). Implementing administrative rules: Utah Admin. Code R277-122 (Board of Education Procurement, eff. Aug. 8, 2024) (USBE adopts Title R33 Purchasing rules with limited exceptions; the rule is silent on multiyear/term limits, so § 1204 controls) (https://schools.utah.gov/adminrules/R277-122); R277-600 (Student Transportation Standards and Procedures, current eff. Feb. 9, 2026) (recognizes that a school district may contract or lease with a third-party provider for pupil transportation; sets service-delivery and reimbursement standards, including for students with disabilities under R277-600-5; does not establish separate contract-term, rebid, or renewal rules) (https://schools.utah.gov/adminrules/R277-600); R277-115 (LEA Supervision and Monitoring Requirements of Third Party Providers and Contracts, eff. July 9, 2024) (silent on contract term, multi-year authorization, renewal procedures, and competitive-bidding requirements) (https://schools.utah.gov/adminrules/R277-115). Note: Utah Code title 41 (and § 41-6a-1304 in particular) grants the Utah Department of Transportation safety-regulation authority over school buses but does not address procurement-contract terms.

districtVariance.label: Unknown
districtVariance.explanation: No state-level evidence of differing statutory interpretation across school districts was located. Sampled district procurement policies (Logan City School District Policy CBG; Garfield County School District Policy CBG; Juab School District Policy CBG; Emery School District Policy DJDG) uniformly recite the 5-year multiyear-contract cap and the written-determination exception verbatim from § 63G-6a-1204(7). The Office of the Utah State Auditor's "Utah Procurement Code: Brief Explanation" confirms that school districts and charter schools are subject to the Utah Procurement Code, and Utah Admin. Code R277-122 incorporates statewide Title R33 procurement rules for USBE. However, dollar-threshold and small-purchase limits below the multiyear cap are explicitly customizable by each district's purchasing policy (the State Auditor cites an example of an entity setting a $350 small-purchase limit instead of $1,000), and the rulemaking authority for a school district is its own board, subject to a nonconflict requirement (§ 63G-6a-103(79)(e)). Localized variation on procurement procedure within statutory limits is therefore structurally permitted, but no district-specific deviation from the 5-year cap itself was found, and no AG opinion or state-auditor finding was located addressing variance in interpretation of the term/rebid rule for student-transportation contracts specifically.

specialEducation.detail: Utah law does not impose different contract-term limits, rebid requirements, procurement rules, or renewal mechanics for special-education / IEP-mandated student transportation. Utah Admin. Code R277-600-5 (Student with Disabilities Transportation) addresses service-delivery and reimbursement: a student with a disability shall be transported on regular buses and regular routes when possible, unless the IEP team determines otherwise; a school district may be reimbursed for the costs of transporting or for alternative transportation for students with disabilities whose severity of disability, or combination of disabilities, necessitates special transportation; and minimum-duration thresholds (15 days during the regular school year / 10 days during ESY) apply to qualifying for reimbursement. The rule does not modify the Utah Procurement Code's contract-term, rebid, or renewal rules. IDEA (20 U.S.C. § 1400 et seq.) and 34 C.F.R. § 300.34(c)(16) require transportation as a related service when the IEP team determines it is needed, but that federal obligation goes to the duty to transport, not to state contracting/procurement rules. The same Utah Code § 63G-6a-1204 framework (5-year cap, rebid at term-end, written-determination exception, conditional-renewal mechanics) therefore applies to special-education / alternative student-transportation contracts. McKinney-Vento and foster-care transportation duties are similarly federally anchored on the duty side and do not alter Utah procurement rules.

perFieldVerdicts.note: § 63G-6a-1204(7)–(8) re-opened from le.utah.gov current version page (C63G-6a-S1204_2021050520210505.html); verbatim text matches Stage 1's claim. The 5-year cap applies to all multiyear contracts including any renewal periods; the carve-outs in (8) (facility, road, public transit project, equipment financing) do not include school-bus services. The written-determination exception in (7) is in the file.

§ 63G-6a-1204(6) re-opened and verified verbatim: "A procurement unit may not continue or renew a multiyear contract after the end of the multiyear contract term or the renewal periods described in the contract, unless the procurement unit engages in a new standard procurement process or complies with an exception, described in this chapter, to using a standard procurement process." Exception statutes (§ 63G-6a-802 sole source/emergency; § 63G-6a-802.7 limited extension; § 63G-6a-506 small purchase; § 63G-6a-507 approved vendor list; § 63G-6a-2105 cooperative procurement) confirmed.

Verified verbatim: § 63G-6a-103(50) defines a multiyear contract to include one permitting renewal without competition beyond year one; § 63G-6a-1204(3)–(5) sets the annual-appropriation and federally-funded conditional-renewal rules; § 63G-6a-1204(7) sets the 5-year ceiling on the contract including renewal periods (written-determination exception); § 63G-6a-802.7 sets the narrow extension-without-procurement authority (re-opened from C63G-6a-S802.7_2020051220200512.html and matches Stage 1's text).

All primary cites re-opened on le.utah.gov. Currentness drift: Stage 1 reported § 63G-6a-103 currency as 5/7/2025 (later 10/14/2025 supersession). The current version of § 63G-6a-103 is effective 3/18/2026, with a further amendment effective 7/1/2026. The cited definitions ((30) educational procurement unit, (50) multiyear contract, (59) procurement unit, (79)(e) rulemaking authority for school districts) are unchanged across these versions. Stage 1's R277-600 currency was October 8, 2024; the current effective date is February 9, 2026 — the rule remains silent on procurement contract term/rebid/renewal, so the substantive answer is unaffected. R277-122 (Aug 8, 2024) and R277-115 (July 9, 2024) re-confirmed.

Unknown verdict supported: sampled district policies (Logan, Garfield, Juab, Emery) uniformly track § 63G-6a-1204 verbatim. No AG opinion, state-auditor finding, or USBE bulletin was located documenting differing interpretation of the term/rebid rule for student-transportation contracts specifically. Procedural-threshold customization (small-purchase limits, written-quote thresholds) is structurally permitted by R277-122 and acknowledged by the State Auditor's brief, but is not evidence of variance in interpretation of the 5-year cap itself.

challengeMatrix.findingChallenged: Max Contract Term — 5 years (including renewal periods), subject to written-determination exception
challengeMatrix.disconfirmingHypothesis: A school-bus or pupil-transportation-specific statute in Title 53G overrides the general 5-year multiyear-contract cap; or § 63G-6a-1204(8) (carve-outs) reaches school-bus services; or a separate education-code provision allows longer terms.
challengeMatrix.challengeSearches: Searched le.utah.gov for Title 53G Chapter 6 and Chapter 7 sections referencing pupil transportation contracts (§§ 53G-6-801 to 803, 53G-7-501, 53G-7-503, 53G-7-606); searched WebSearch for "Utah Code Title 53G school district pupil transportation contract competitive bid term"; opened § 53G-6-803 (which is "Parental right to academic accommodations" — unrelated). Re-opened § 63G-6a-1204(8) verbatim: carve-outs are "design or construction of a facility, a road, a public transit project, or a contract for the financing of equipment" — none reaches private school-bus services.
challengeMatrix.result: No school-bus-specific procurement statute that overrides § 63G-6a-1204 was located. Carve-outs in (8) are narrow and inapplicable.

challengeMatrix.disconfirmingHypothesis: An exception permits a school district to renew or continue a student-transportation contract at term-end without a new standard procurement process — e.g., sole source, cooperative procurement, evergreen rollover, board waiver, or piggyback authority — that would convert the answer from "Required" to "Conditional" or "Not Required."
challengeMatrix.challengeSearches: Re-opened § 63G-6a-1204(6) (rebid mandate); § 63G-6a-802 (exceptions including sole source, emergency); § 63G-6a-802.7 (limited extension without standard procurement, generally ≤ 120 days); § 63G-6a-2105 (cooperative procurement); § 63G-6a-506 (small purchase); § 63G-6a-507 (approved vendor list). Confirmed these are exceptions to the standard procurement process, not blanket waivers of the rebid mandate at term-end — they are themselves authorized procurement methods.
challengeMatrix.result: Exceptions exist but they are limited and procedurally constrained; the default rule is "Required." The Stage 1 label "Required" is correct; the exceptions are documented in the rule statement.

challengeMatrix.findingChallenged: Renewal Mechanics — five-year ceiling with annual-appropriation contingency and limited extension authority; no statutory CPI/fuel-index/automatic rollover
challengeMatrix.disconfirmingHypothesis: An evergreen-rollover, automatic-renewal, CPI/fuel-escalator, or board-waiver mechanism is statutorily authorized for student-transportation contracts.
challengeMatrix.challengeSearches: Searched § 63G-6a-1204 verbatim (no auto-renewal language); confirmed § 63G-6a-103(35) authorizes "fixed price contract with price adjustment" — but this is a permissible contract type, not a duration override. Confirmed § 63G-6a-802.7 is the only extension-without-procurement authority and it is narrow (avoid lapse in critical service, protest/appeal period, generally ≤ 120 days). Reviewed R277-600 and R277-115 (no contract-term or renewal-mechanic provisions). No automatic-rollover, evergreen, or CPI-escalator mechanism prescribed by statute.
challengeMatrix.result: No statutory CPI/fuel-index/evergreen mechanism exists; renewal periods are permitted within the 5-year envelope subject to the annual-appropriation contingency and the (7) written-determination exception for longer terms.

challengeMatrix.disconfirmingHypothesis: Utah law sets different contract-term limits, rebid requirements, procurement rules, or renewal mechanics for special-education / IEP / McKinney-Vento / foster-care / disability transportation.
challengeMatrix.challengeSearches: Re-opened R277-600-5 (Student with Disabilities Transportation) verbatim via Cornell LII and confirmed via schools.utah.gov: the rule addresses service-delivery, IEP-team determination, and reimbursement (15-day / 10-day route minimums) but does not set contract-term, rebid, or renewal rules. Searched WebSearch for "Utah school district pupil transportation contract bid special education IEP separate procurement" — no separate procurement statute found. Searched for McKinney-Vento and foster-care transportation procurement carve-outs in Utah — none located; federal obligations are duty-side, not procurement-side.
challengeMatrix.result: No separate special-education procurement rules located. The general § 63G-6a-1204 framework applies.

challengeMatrix.disconfirmingHypothesis: Affirmative state-level evidence exists (AG opinion, state-auditor finding, USBE bulletin, or USBA guidance) that either documents uniform statewide application of § 63G-6a-1204 to student-transportation contracts (which would shift Variance to "No") or documents differing district interpretations of the term/rebid rule (which would shift Variance to "Yes").
challengeMatrix.challengeSearches: Searched WebSearch for "Utah attorney general opinion 63G-6a-1204 multiyear contract school district" — no AG opinion on point. Reviewed at least four district policies (Logan, Garfield, Juab, Emery) — all uniformly recite the 5-year cap; none deviates. Reviewed the State Auditor's "Utah Procurement Code: Brief Explanation" — confirms applicability of the Procurement Code to school districts and procedural-threshold customization, but does not affirmatively confirm uniform interpretation of the 5-year cap.
challengeMatrix.result: Sampled district policies are uniform with statute, but no affirmative state-level evidence of either uniform interpretation or variance for student-transportation contracts specifically was located. "Unknown" remains the most accurate label.
challengeMatrix.confidenceImpact: Confirms High confidence (caveat: the residual uncertainty is on the variance question itself, which is recorded as Unknown, not on the underlying statutory rule).

challengeMatrix.findingChallenged: Currentness — code-currency dates and version numbers
challengeMatrix.disconfirmingHypothesis: § 63G-6a-1204 or § 63G-6a-103 was amended or superseded after the dates Stage 1 recorded, in a way that changes the substantive rule.
challengeMatrix.challengeSearches: Pulled bills-affecting page for § 63G-6a-1204 (empty — no pending bills). Re-opened § 63G-6a-1204 current version page (C63G-6a-S1204_2021050520210505.html, effective 5/5/2021, amended by Ch. 64, 2021 General Session) — unchanged. Re-opened § 63G-6a-103 — current version effective 3/18/2026 (Stage 1 reported 5/7/2025; this is a currentness drift) with another version effective 7/1/2026; (30), (50), (59), and (79)(e) are unchanged in both. R277-600 current effective date is February 9, 2026 (Stage 1 said October 8, 2024) — but the rule remains silent on procurement contract term/rebid/renewal, so substance is unchanged.
challengeMatrix.result: Currentness drift identified for § 63G-6a-103 and R277-600 metadata; substantive rule unchanged. Stage 1 dates updated in this pack.
challengeMatrix.confidenceImpact: No impact on substantive verdicts; metadata corrected.

sourcesUnreachable: le.utah.gov 'master' pages (e.g., 63G-6a-S1204.html, 63G-6a-S103.html) returned only navigation via WebFetch (200 HTTP) — accessed verbatim text via dated 'C'-prefixed current-version pages with a browser User-Agent via curl. No source was lost; this is the documented Utah Code pattern.

adminrules.utah.gov/public/rule/R277-600/Current Rules returned 404 to WebFetch when called with the encoded space; substance was retrieved from schools.utah.gov/adminrules/R277-600 and cross-verified via Cornell LII.

sourceLedger.codeCurrency: Current version effective 5/5/2021; last amended by Ch. 64, 2021 General Session. Bills-affecting page (le.utah.gov) returned no pending bills.
sourceLedger.fieldsSupported: Max Term; Rebid Required; Renewal Mechanics
sourceLedger.relevantText: (6) A procurement unit may not continue or renew a multiyear contract after the end of the multiyear contract term or the renewal periods described in the contract, unless the procurement unit engages in a new standard procurement process or complies with an exception, described in this chapter, to using a standard procurement process. (7) A multiyear contract, including any renewal periods, may not exceed a period of five years, unless: (a) the procurement official determines, in writing, that: (i) a longer period is necessary in order to obtain the procurement item; (ii) a longer period is customary for industry standards; or (iii) a longer period is in the best interest of the procurement unit; and (b) the written determination described in Subsection (7)(a) is included in the file relating to the procurement. (8) This section does not apply to a contract for the design or construction of a facility, a road, a public transit project, or a contract for the financing of equipment. (3) annual-appropriation contingency for renewal; (4)–(5) federal-funds conditional-renewal rules.

sourceLedger.citation: Utah Code Ann. § 63G-6a-103 (Definitions), subsections (30) "Educational procurement unit," (50) "Multiyear contract," (59) "Procurement unit," (79)(e) Rulemaking authority for school district
sourceLedger.officialUrl: https://le.utah.gov/xcode/Title63G/Chapter6A/63G-6a-S103.html (current dated version: https://le.utah.gov/xcode/Title63G/Chapter6A/C63G-6a-S103_2026031820260318.html; forthcoming version eff. 7/1/2026 at C63G-6a-S103_2026050620260701.html)
sourceLedger.codeCurrency: Current version effective 3/18/2026 (superseded 7/1/2026 by a later amendment that does not alter the cited definitions).
sourceLedger.fieldsSupported: Applicability of the Procurement Code to school districts (Statutory Citation, District Variance)
sourceLedger.relevantText: (30) "Educational procurement unit" means: (a) a school district; (b) a public school, including a local school board or a charter school; (c) the Utah Schools for the Deaf and the Blind; (d) the Utah Education and Telehealth Network; (e) an institution of higher education of the state described in Section 53H-1-102; or (f) the State Board of Education. (50) "Multiyear contract" means a contract that extends beyond a one-year period, including a contract that permits renewal of the contract, without competition, beyond the first year of the contract. (59) "Procurement unit" includes … (d) an educational procurement unit. (79)(e) for a school district or a public school, [rulemaking authority is] the board, except to the extent of a school district's own nonadministrative rules that do not conflict with the provisions of this chapter.

sourceLedger.citation: Utah Code Ann. § 63G-6a-802.7 (Extension of a contract without engaging in a standard procurement process)
sourceLedger.codeCurrency: Effective 5/12/2020; amended by Ch. 257, 2020 General Session
sourceLedger.relevantText: A procurement official may extend an existing contract without engaging in a standard procurement process: (1) for a period not to exceed 120 days, if extension is necessary to avoid a lapse in a critical government service or to mitigate harm to public health, safety, welfare, or property, with delineated triggers; (2) for the period of a protest, appeal, or court action; or (3) for a period exceeding 120 days only after consulting the attorney general or unit's attorney and making a written determination of no antitrust violation.

sourceLedger.fieldsSupported: Renewal Mechanics (context: multiple-award contracts under standard procurement)
sourceLedger.relevantText: Through a standard procurement process, the division or an independent procurement unit may enter into multiple award contracts with multiple persons. The 5-year multiyear cap still applies via § 1204(7).

sourceLedger.citation: Utah Code Ann. § 63G-6a-2105 (Participation of a public entity or a procurement unit in agreements or contracts of procurement units — Cooperative purchasing — State cooperative contracts)
sourceLedger.fieldsSupported: Rebid Required (exception); Renewal Mechanics (cooperative procurement context)
sourceLedger.relevantText: Authorizes the chief procurement officer and procurement units to participate in, sponsor, conduct, or administer cooperative procurements with other Utah procurement units or public entities, subject to participating-addendum and notice conditions.

sourceLedger.sourceTier: Tier 1 (negative result) — Title 53G education statute reviewed for school-bus-specific procurement rule
sourceLedger.citation: Utah Code Ann. § 53G-6-803 (Parental right to academic accommodations)
sourceLedger.fieldsSupported: (none) — confirms there is no school-bus-specific procurement statute at this citation, contrary to occasional secondary-source misreads.
sourceLedger.relevantText: Section addresses parental rights to reasonable academic accommodations; no provision on transportation contracts.

sourceLedger.citation: Utah Admin. Code R277-122 (Board of Education Procurement)
sourceLedger.fieldsSupported: Statutory Citation; Renewal Mechanics (procurement procedure for USBE; small-purchase thresholds)
sourceLedger.relevantText: Establishes procurement procedures for USBE by incorporating Title R33 (Purchasing and General Services, as in effect April 1, 2022) with specific exceptions. Sets small-purchase / quote thresholds. Rule is silent on contract term limits, multi-year authorization, and renewal procedures; § 63G-6a-1204 controls.

sourceLedger.citation: Utah Admin. Code R277-600 (Student Transportation Standards and Procedures), including R277-600-3 (General Provisions) and R277-600-5 (Student with Disabilities Transportation)
sourceLedger.codeCurrency: Current effective date February 9, 2026 (Stage 1 reported October 8, 2024 — currentness drift corrected; substance unchanged)
sourceLedger.fieldsSupported: Special-education differentiation; baseline authority for districts to contract with third-party providers
sourceLedger.relevantText: Authorizes school districts to contract or lease with a third-party provider for pupil transportation services. R277-600-5: a student with a disability shall be transported on regular buses and regular routes when possible unless the IEP team determines otherwise; reimbursement available for specialized transport; minimum-duration thresholds (15 days regular school year / 10 days ESY). Rule is silent on contract term, rebid, and renewal mechanics — procurement is governed by Chapter 63G-6a.

sourceLedger.citation: Utah Admin. Code R277-115 (LEA Supervision and Monitoring Requirements of Third Party Providers and Contracts)
sourceLedger.fieldsSupported: Special-education differentiation (third-party-provider oversight is uniform); negative result on procurement term rules
sourceLedger.relevantText: Imposes background-check, monitoring, documentation, and termination-notice obligations on LEAs using third-party providers. Rule is silent on contract term limits, multi-year authorization, renewal procedures, and competitive-bidding requirements; only restriction in subsection (8) is that funds may not encumber services beyond contract length.

sourceLedger.citation: Office of the Utah State Auditor, "Utah Procurement Code: Brief Explanation"
sourceLedger.codeCurrency: Brief Explanation as published (revised 9/28/2020 PDF posted)
sourceLedger.relevantText: School Districts and Charter Schools are subject to the Utah Procurement Code and Administrative Rule R33 (except to the extent of a school district's own nonadministrative rules that do not conflict with the Code). Internal procedural thresholds (small purchase / written quote / public advertising) are customizable by each entity, illustrating that statutory term/rebid rules are uniform while internal procedural thresholds may vary.

sourceLedger.citation: Logan City School District Policy CBG — Procurement: Contracts and Contract Limitations
sourceLedger.fieldsSupported: District Variance (uniform adoption of the 5-year cap)
sourceLedger.relevantText: Quotes the 5-year cap verbatim: "A multiyear contract, including any renewal periods, may not exceed a period of five years, unless the Procurement Officer makes a written determination that the longer period is necessary in order to obtain the procurement item, or that a longer period is customary for industry standards, or that a longer period is in the best interest of the District." Adopts the written-determination exception without local modification.

sourceLedger.citation: Garfield County School District Policy CBG — Procurement: Contracts and Contract Limitations
sourceLedger.fieldsSupported: District Variance (uniform adoption of the 5-year cap)
sourceLedger.relevantText: Adopts the 5-year multiyear-contract cap with the written-determination exception, identical in substance to statute and to Logan City SD's policy. Does not contain student-transportation-specific carve-outs.

sourceLedger.citation: Juab School District Policy CBG — Procurement: Contracts and Contract Limitations
sourceLedger.fieldsSupported: District Variance (uniform adoption of the 5-year cap)
sourceLedger.relevantText: Identifies the 5-year multiyear-contract cap and written-determination exception consistent with statute.

sourceLedger.citation: Emery School District Policy DJDG — Procurement: Contracts and Contract Limitations
sourceLedger.fieldsSupported: District Variance (uniform adoption of the 5-year cap, including in a small/rural district)
sourceLedger.relevantText: Adopts the 5-year multiyear-contract cap with the written-determination exception, supporting uniform statewide adoption.

sourceLedger.sourceTier: Tier 1 (negative result) — Transportation safety statute outside the Procurement Code
sourceLedger.citation: Utah Code Ann. tit. 41 (Motor Vehicles) and § 41-6a-1304 (School buses — Rules regarding design and operation)
sourceLedger.fieldsSupported: (none) — confirms transportation safety regulation is separate from procurement
sourceLedger.relevantText: USBE pupil-transportation page notes pupil-transportation safety authority is under Title 41 of the Utah Code (the Department of Transportation regulates school-bus design/operation, whether buses are owned by a district or operated under contract). Title 41 does not address contract term, rebid, or renewal mechanics.

verificationNotes.searchStrategy: Stage-2 verification combined targeted statutory re-opens (le.utah.gov via curl with browser User-Agent to reach JS-loaded dated 'C'-prefixed current-version pages where WebFetch returned only navigation), WebFetch on agency / administrative-rule landing pages (schools.utah.gov, adminrules.utah.gov, auditor.utah.gov), and WebSearch for AG opinions, auditor findings, and bills-affecting checks. Pathfinder-only sources (FindLaw, Justia, Cornell LII) were used solely to locate the official citation; the official le.utah.gov URLs are recorded as the primary citations.
verificationNotes.currentLawCheck: § 63G-6a-1204: current version effective 5/5/2021 (last amended by Ch. 64, 2021 General Session); bills-affecting page returned no pending bills. § 63G-6a-103: current version effective 3/18/2026 (a 5/7/2025 → 10/14/2025 → 3/18/2026 sequence post-dates Stage 1's metadata; cited definitions (30), (50), (59), (79)(e) are unchanged across these versions and in the forthcoming 7/1/2026 version). § 63G-6a-802.7: effective 5/12/2020 (Ch. 257, 2020 General Session). Utah Admin. Code R277-122: effective August 8, 2024. R277-600: current effective date February 9, 2026 (Stage 1 reported October 8, 2024 — currentness drift). R277-115: effective July 9, 2024.
verificationNotes.unresolvedIssues: District-Level Interpretation Variance remains Unknown: no AG opinion, state-auditor finding, or USBE bulletin was located that affirmatively documents either uniform or differing interpretation of § 63G-6a-1204 across districts for student-transportation contracts specifically. Four sampled district policies (Logan, Garfield, Juab, Emery) all recite the 5-year cap verbatim and add no transportation-specific carve-out, but the absence of evidence of variance is not affirmative evidence of uniformity. The variance label remains "Unknown" rather than "No" pending an authoritative state-level data point.
verificationNotes.conflictChecks: Checked whether any school-bus-specific Title 53G provision overrides § 63G-6a-1204 — none found. Checked whether § 63G-6a-1204(8) carve-outs reach student-transportation services — they do not (limited to facility, road, public-transit project, equipment financing). Checked whether R277-600 / R277-115 / R277-122 imposes a separate term/rebid/renewal regime — all are silent. Checked AG opinions and auditor reports for variance — none located on point. No authoritative conflict remains.

notesSpecialProvisions: Several Utah Procurement Code provisions and related rules materially shape the practical application of the five fields for student-transportation contracts: (1) Small-purchase / dollar-threshold rules — under R277-122 and § 63G-6a-506, Utah procurement units (including school districts) may use a small-purchase process below statutory thresholds; an individual school district may set lower internal small-purchase limits than the State allows (the State Auditor cites an example of an entity using $350 instead of $1,000), but cannot exceed statutory ceilings. (2) Sole source / emergency procurement — § 63G-6a-802 authorizes procurement without a standard procurement process in cases of sole source (Subsection (1)(a)) or emergency, and is the principal statutory route for a school district to avoid an otherwise required rebid at term-end. (3) Cooperative purchasing / piggyback authority — § 63G-6a-2105 permits a procurement unit (including a school district) to participate in a cooperative procurement with other Utah procurement units or public entities, subject to participating-addendum and notice conditions; this can substitute for a standalone competitive procurement at term-end. (4) Approved vendor list — § 63G-6a-507 (referenced in § 63G-6a-103(3)) authorizes use of an approved-vendor-list process. (5) Limited extension without procurement — § 63G-6a-802.7 allows a procurement official to extend an existing contract without a standard procurement process for ≤120 days to avoid a lapse in a critical government service or for the period of a protest/appeal, and for longer than 120 days only after consulting the attorney general or unit's attorney and making a written determination. (6) Annual-appropriation contingency — § 63G-6a-1204(3) makes any multiyear contract conditional on continued appropriations after year one (with parallel federal-funds rules in subsections (4)–(5)). (7) Charter-school rules — charter schools are explicitly within the Procurement Code as "educational procurement units" (§ 63G-6a-103(30)(b)) and the State Auditor's brief confirms charter schools are subject to the Code. (8) Special-education differentiation — none under Utah law; R277-600-5 governs service-delivery and reimbursement for students with disabilities but does not modify procurement contract mechanics, so § 63G-6a-1204's 5-year cap, rebid mandate, and conditional-renewal rules apply identically to IEP-mandated, McKinney-Vento, foster-care, and alternative student-transportation contracts. (9) State-approval requirements — USBE's R277-600 requires Superintendent approval of bus routes and reimbursement (operational, not procurement-contract-term). (10) Title 41 safety overlay — § 41-6a-1304 and Title 41 broadly give UDOT safety-regulation authority over school buses operated by or under contract with a school district, independent of and unaffected by the Procurement Code.
exceptionsUnresolved: All five fields resolved for Utah, with one residual uncertainty preserved in District-Level Interpretation Variance: the variance label is "Unknown" rather than "No" because no AG opinion, state-auditor finding, or USBE bulletin was located that affirmatively documents uniform statewide interpretation of § 63G-6a-1204 for student-transportation contracts; conversely, no evidence of differing district interpretation was found. Sampled district policies (Logan, Garfield, Juab, Emery) uniformly recite the 5-year cap and add no transportation-specific carve-out, but the absence of contrary evidence is not affirmative evidence of uniformity, so "Unknown" is the more accurate label. Substantive Max-Term, Rebid, Renewal, Statutory-Citation, and Special-Education fields are confirmed by primary statutory sources and are not unresolved.
